data.title: National Vision Holdings: Rating Upgrade As Turnaround Strategy Worked
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about National Vision Holdings (NASDAQ:EYE) previously in December last year with a hold rating because I wasn’t sure if the turnaround efforts would work. Fast forward to today, the story has changed. The turnaround plan is not only working but also delivering faster-than-expected results, driving both sales acceleration and margin expansion. While the stock has already rallied meaningfully, I see scope for further upside supported by the managed care strategy, strong pricing and product execution, and two powerful growth catalysts set to launch in 2H25 that are not yet embedded in guidance.

2Q25 earnings

EYE’s 2Q25 results marked a clear and positive inflection point. The transformation initiatives that caused execution uncertainty have now delivered tangible and seemingly faster-than-expected results. Net revenue from continuing operations grew 7.7% y/y to $486.4 million. This was driven by adj. comparable store sales growth [CSSG] of 5.9%, the tenth straight quarter of positive comps, and a sharp acceleration from 2.4% in 2Q24. Growth momentum was broad-based. America’s Best delivered a 6.3% CSSG, while Eyeglass World saw a 2.8% CSSG, its best 1H performance since 2021, which signals a solid turnaround. Down the P&L, adj. EBIT grew 69.1% y/y to $23.8 million, led by a 180 bps margin expansion to 4.9% (the highest in recent years). This combination shows that the turnaround action is indeed translating into tangible earnings growth. Management also raised FY25 guidance, which I see as a strong sign of confidence. To me, 2Q25 results clearly signal that EYE’s transformation is working with continued momentum.

Managed Care Strategy

Previously, my view on the managed care strategy was that it was promising but unproven. We now have evidence that this strategy is working, and I expect it to be EYE’s primary growth driver moving forward. As a recap on this strategy, here is a quote from my previous update:

First, and arguably one of the most important drivers of future growth, is EYE’s success in shifting its focus toward managed care customers. In FY24, managed care penetration increased to 40% of total revenue, up from 35% in FY23, and continues to grow at a high single-digit percentage rate. The target is to have this become ~50% of revenue mix over the next few years. The reason I say this is important is because managed care customers are higher value customers. These customers typically have insurance coverage that reduces out-of-pocket costs (ie they pay less), which, I believe, makes them less price-sensitive compared to the typical customer that pays paying entirely out of pocket. Additionally, insurance policies often encourage regular check-ups and eyewear purchases, increasing the likelihood of recurring transactions. This makes EYE’s business a lot more stable (in terms of revenue visibility) and also gives it much more flexibility to raise prices as managed care customers have higher affordability (since the way they perceive cost is by how much they pay out of pocket). Furthermore, since demand is less elastic, it means EYE can reduce reliance on aggressive discounting, which is margin accretive.

To put things into perspective, the managed care customer segment saw strong low-double-digit CCSG, driven by both traffic and average ticket. This solid development effectively confirms that EYE is attracting these targeted customer groups—higher-value, insured customers who are less price-sensitive and tend to transact more frequently. I didn’t touch on the financial impact previously because there was a lack of data points (and frankly, I didn’t even know it would work), but this aspect of things deserves attention in today’s context. Positively, the financial impact is meaningful. Management noted a 2% y/y mix shift from the cash-pay cohort to managed care, and the way I see it, this is a structural tailwind for both ticket size and margins for as long as this managed care strategy continues to work. Also, insured customers typically have benefits that support larger purchases, so it directly improves EYE’s revenue quality and unit economics. We know that in FY24, managed care penetration was 40%, up from 35% in FY23; using the same pace, it should be ~42.5% as of 2Q25. As the mix moves toward management’s goal of 50% revenue, this should continue to lift CSSG in the foreseeable future.

Pricing and Assortment Initiatives Resonate Broadly

In my last write-up, I was worried that consumer discretionary spending would be impacted by the macro environment, and with that in mind, raising prices should be difficult for EYE. I was wrong again. The 6.6% y/y increase in average ticket was the major contributor, and in my opinion, that reflects very well on EYE’s pricing and product assortment strategy. EYE did not just raise prices bluntly; it did combine the price increase with an improvement in value proposition. What I mean by that is EYE's decision to broaden its product assortment to include more desirable, higher-end designer brands that customers are actively choosing. This is a really smart move, as it better aligns the product mix with the purchasing power of the managed care customer base (better spending power), who often have insurance benefits that make these premium frames highly accessible with little to no out-of-pocket cost.

With this strategy, by the end of 2025, management expects ~40% of frames to be priced at $99 or more, which is a steep increase from the 20% at the end of 4Q24. I am optimistic about this move, as early results are encouraging. New designer lines like L.A.M.B. and Ted Baker are selling at above-average rates, and upcoming launches of Jimmy Choo and Hugo Boss should continue to support this premium momentum. Importantly, the “trade-up” trend is not isolated to insured customers; it was interesting to also see cash-pay customers gravitating toward higher-priced options.

Future Growth Catalysts Provide a Conservative Outlook

Looking ahead, I see potential for growth to further accelerate as several major growth drivers have not even gone live. This makes the raised FY25 guidance look conservative. Firstly, the guide excludes any contribution from two major catalysts set for 2H25, which could very well extend momentum into 2026. The first is a brand refresh for America’s Best with the new “Every Eye Deserves Better” campaign. This is a modernization strategy aimed at attracting higher-income customers. This strategy is important because it allows EYE to shift its media approach beyond its historical reliance on TV and search marketing toward mid-funnel channels like social and digital media. These are the channels where young and modern consumers are more active. Positively, this strategy is already showing early signs of success, as it has contributed to growth with customers in higher income brackets, particularly in the $75,000 to $100,000 range.

The second is the rollout of a new CRM platform with Adobe. I see this as a critical upgrade that shifts marketing from “one-to-many” to “one-to-one.” In other words, EYE’s marketing is now personalized and targeted. Relative to the past, where nearly all customers received the same messaging regardless of their profile, this new platform enables highly precise targeting and audience selection, allowing for personalized marketing based on customer demographics, income levels, and past purchase history.

Altogether, both of these should materially improve acquisition and retention. The fact that these initiatives are not yet factored into guidance just seems to me that management is sandbagging the numbers.

Valuation

Yes, valuation has jumped from the lows of ~20x NTM PE to 30x today, and although this may look like the stock has “overrun” its course, I think there is still upside from here. From a valuation perspective, EYE is not expensive at all relative to history. At 30x, this is just slightly above the past 3-years average, and to be fair, the upgrade in multiples is justified given the improvement in CSSG. From here, we do not need further expansion in multiples to justify a positive upside as long as EYE can grow its earnings, which I think it can. 2Q25 literally grew almost 100% for reference, and with the managed care strategy working and two strong catalysts in 2H25, I am optimistic that EPS can grow as consensus expected. As you can see, the FY28 EPS estimate is $1.24, which equates to a share price of $32 even if we expect multiples to see mean revision.

Risk

There are risks to my bullish thesis. The biggest is that growth is still leaning heavily on average ticket increase, while overall traffic remains slightly negative (down 0.4% in 2Q25). If the upcoming marketing and CRM rollouts fail to drive traffic improvements, growth could slow as EYE laps last year’s sizable pricing actions. On top of that, the uncertain macro backdrop could pressure the core value-focused customer, making any meaningful traffic rebound harder to achieve.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for EYE, as I think the business is at a clear inflection point. The managed care strategy is working very well, and its pricing initiatives are resonating with both insured and cash-pay customers. The upcoming marketing and CRM rollouts offer potential upside to the already raised guidance. Valuation is also reasonable relative to history, and based on my model, earnings growth alone can drive upside from here.

data.title: Intellia Therapeutics: It May Be Time To Get Excited And Buy (Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

When I last covered Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:NTLA) for Seeking Alpha at the end of May, the share price of the Cambridge, Massachusetts-based pharma company, developing gene therapies using CRISPR technology, was tanking. This was after it reported that a patient with ATTR-CM (transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy) being treated with its candidate nexiguran ziclumeran ("nex-z"), had experienced grade 4 liver transaminase elevations.

Grade 4 safety events are categorized as "life threatening or disabling adverse events," and can put an end to clinical studies of a drug candidate and lead to its being shelved - this explains why Intellia's stock fell from ~$10 per share, to <$7, on the news.

For example, Pfizer (PFE) ended development of its oral GLP-1 drug danuglipron after instances of elevated liver enzymes were observed in a patient. Given that Intellia's candidate is a gene therapy that makes permanent edits to patients' DNA, fears may have been heightened. However, Intellia noted its patients issues "appear to be resolving without hospitalization or medical intervention and have fallen to grade 3 ALT and grade 2 AST elevations," and on its Q2 earnings call which took place last week, CEO John Leonard told analysts the company has made the:

decision to increase enrollment to approximately 1,200 patients in magnitude our Phase III study evaluating nex-z in ATTR cardiomyopathy subject to health authority review.

I assigned stock a Hold rating in my last note, concluding that:

Clearly, to grow its valuation, Intellia must secure approval for one of its assets - the company has racked up an accumulated deficit of ~$2.3bn, and has ~$700m of cash and near-term at its disposal, against a net loss of $(114m) in Q1 2025, versus $(107m) in the prior year.

The bear thesis is that so long as there is uncertainty on safety, Intellia's drugs are unapprovable, and cash will be swiftly burned through, leaving investors empty-handed. The bull thesis is that the patient's issues quickly resolved and that one or perhaps two drugs could therefore be approved within the next 2-3 years, and claim best-in-class status.

So far, its the bull thesis that has played out - Intellia stock is up >45% since my note, presently trading at $10.9 per share- up >10% on a six-month basis, but down 52% on a 1-year basis, and 45% on a five year basis.

In this note I plan to update on Intellia's plans to carry out three commercial launches for its two late-stage assets, Lonvo-z, and Nex-z, beginning in 2027, and highlight the "make-or-break catalysts the company has upcoming later this year.

Let's begin with a quick overview of the business, and Q2 earnings and business updates.

Intellia - State Of Play After Q2 Earnings

In Q2, Intellia earned $14.2m of collaboration revenues, primarily related to its collaboration deal with New York Pharma giant Regeneron (REGN), who according to Intellia's latest 10Q filing:

shares in approximately 25% of worldwide development costs and commercial profits for the ATTR program and has an option to enter into a co-promotion agreement for the U.S. commercialization.

R&D expenses came to $97m, and G&A expenses $27.2m, resulting in a net loss for the quarter of $(101.3m), versus $(147m) in the prior year period.

In the first six months of 2025, Intellia reported a net loss of $(215.6m), and as of the end of Q2, a cash position of $631m - the current rate of burn, Intellia's cash reserves would be exhausted by the end of 2026.

The above slide taken from an August corporate presentation describes Intellia's two lead candidates, which have been more than a decade in the making - Intellia completed its initial public offering ("IPO") in May, 2016, raising ~$113m at $18 per share.

As we can see, Intellia hopes to file for approval for Lonvo-x in hereditary angioedema - a disease characterized by recurrent episodes of severe swelling, typically involving the limbs, face, intestinal tracts and airway - via a Biologics License Application ("BLA") in 2026. It has dosed a first patient in a pivotal Phase 3 study and intends to share longer-term data from its Phase 1/2 study later this year.

For Nex-z, the target indications are ATTR with polyneuropathy, and ATTR with cardiomyopathy, the latter being the far larger market, the disease itself defined by the Cleveland Clinic as follows:

Transthyretin amyloidosis (ATTR-CM) occurs when your liver produces faulty transthyretin (TTR) proteins. Clumps of these abnormal proteins (called fibrils) build up in your heart’s main pumping chamber. Your left ventricle becomes stiff and weak. This cardiomyopathy makes it harder for your heart to pump blood to your body. Heart failure can occur.

Analysis - Assessing Approval Prospects In HAE

It should be noted that several drugs are approved to HAE, and ATTR-PN, and ATTR-CM.

In the first indication, you have Biocryst's (BCRX) oral drug Orladeyo, which is forecast by management to earn ~$590m revenues in 2025, Takeda Pharma's (TAK) injectable Kallikrein inhibitor (similar MoA to Orladeyo) Takhzyro, which earned $374m in the first quarter of 2024, up 35% year-on-year.

Meanwhile, Kalvista Pharma (KALV) recently secured approval for Ekterly (sebetralstat), also an oral plasma kallikrein inhibitor, while Pharvaris (PHVS) has an oral bradykinin B2-receptor antagonist progressing through clinical studies.

Intellia's candidate has a potentially major advantage, being a "one and done" gene therapy that could offer a permanent cure for HAE after a single infusion at a physician's office, and as illustrated below, Intellia believes its efficacy profile is "best-in-class."

While the "one and done" nature of a gene therapy is attractive, the therapy, given it is making permanent edits to genes, must be safe. In its Phase 2 study, no patients experienced a serious adverse event, with the most common events being headache and fatigue, experienced by 40% and 30% of the 25mg dose arm, and 36% and 27% in the 50mg arm. Intellia also noted that one patient "had transient Grade 2 increase in ALT on Day 22."

Intellia noted on its earnings call, in relation to its Phase 3 study of Lonvo-z:

When you consider that the HAELO study requires 60 patients to complete enrollment, it is notable that we screened 41 patients in April alone. This degree of demand in our study is remarkable. And has enabled us to stop recruiting during the second quarter a mere 4 months after dosing the first patient. A majority of these patients are coming off of leading therapies, including lanadelumab, which we believe supports the underappreciated degree of unmet need.

We aspire to reset expectations and the standard of care for patients living with this debilitating disease to achieve 0 attacks in most of the patients without the need for any HAE medication. We look forward to sharing additional data from our ongoing Phase I/II trial later this year.

Personally, I find these updates impressive, as it shows that patients and physicians consider Lonvo-x to be a safe and effective drug, which the Phase 2 results, albeit from small patient populations - 10 and 11 in the 25mg and 50mg cohorts only - support, and shows they may well be willing to switch from current standards of care.

Doubtless, Lonvo-x will be priced at a significant premium to rival drugs due to it's one-time use. With 6k patients in the U.S., if the drug was priced at - let's speculate $500k, treating 33% of the patient population ought to secure "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) revenues, although presumably, if the drug did provide a permanent cure, the patient population would start to dwindle, similarly to the Gilead Sciences (GILD) Solvani and Harvoni, which effectively cure hepatitis C. Double-digit billion dollar selling assets in their prime, their revenues eroded rapidly once there were no more patients left to treat.

ATTR Amyloidosis - Chance To Introduce A New Standard-of-Care

ATTR-CM patients number ~20k in the U.S., Intellia says, and there are presently three approved medicines - Pfizer's (PFE) Vyndaqel, $5.5bn revenues in 2024, and more recently approved (in March this year) in ATTR-CM, Alnylam's (ALNY) Amvuttra - $492m revenues in Q2 - the drug is also approved to treat ATTR-PN, earning $230m in Q2 2024. Finally, Bridge Bio's Attruby was approved in ATTR-CM in November last year, and earned $72m last quarter.

With the MAGNITUDE Phase 3 study seemingly back on track after its safety setback, and MAGNITUDE-2 progressing in ATTR-PN, there appears to be a compelling opportunity in play to transform the standard-of-care ("SoC") in these indications. In the below slide, Intellia shows how Nex-z stacked up against amvuttra (vutrisiran) and attruby (acoramidis).

The drug has also stabilized patients in measures such as 6-minute walk test, while data (which needs confirming, perhaps via the data set due this year) also supports the thesis that Nex-z may be superior to current SoC's in terms of time to first cardiovacular event.

Intellia says the ATTR market opportunity will rise to ~$12bn by 2028, by which time it's possible Nex-z could be on the market. If it is safe, then surely it is guaranteed "blockbuster" revenues, albeit safety is harder to measure than in HAE due to the more severe nature of ATTR.

As we can see, instances of cardiac failure, and myocardial infarction have been reported, and even a death, however the Phase 3 study endpoint in ATTR-CM is a composite endpoint of CV-related mortality and CV-related events, and inevitably some patients will experience disease progression - everything depends on the drug's performance against the three approved therapies. As Intellia's CEO put it on the earnings call last week:

Expanding the patient number in the study will provide a more robust data set particularly in the stabilizer stratum, which we know will be very important to patients, clinicians and payers. We believe nex-z in combination with the stabilizer will provide meaningful clinical benefits beyond treating with only a stabilizer, which will be a key differentiator in the commercial setting.

Now with the benefit of recent clinical readouts, we know how to best capitalize on the rapid, deep and consistent TTR reduction achieved with nex-z to make it into a formidable and differentiated competitor in this large and growing market.

The expansion of the trial will surely increase Intellia's R&D costs, and, I am speculating slightly here, but I wonder if the patient population may have been enlarged to avoid the smaller population failing to achieve a statistically significant result? On the earnings call Chief Medical Officer David Lebwohl provided an explanation I found plausible, however, as follows:

With a very brisk enrollment to the trial, we also saw that we could enroll 1,200 patients in the time frame in which we've guided to complete enrollment. And that number really looked optimal in terms of the timing of both the final analysis and an interim analysis that will be taking place. But this is -- gets us there to analysis faster because you have more patients, more event securing, but also it's not so many patients that you're -- you're waiting a long time for the enrollment period. So that's where we got to. And we think this will be -- and as you've also heard it maintains our runway. So putting that all together, this is a number we chose to actually see this size is similar to other studies that are out there.

Concluding Thoughts Catalysts Due 2025 May Pave The Way For Momentous Few Years

In general, CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP) has always been my preferred company in the gene editing field, because of the partnerships it has been able to secure, and the fact it secured a historic first approval for a Sickle Cell Disease ("SCD") / beta thalassemia ("TDT") therapy in Casgevy.

Casgevy is an ex-vivo therapy however and the lengthy period of time it takes to collect patient's cells, engineer them, and reinfuse them - not to mention the safety issues involved - has held back sales of that drug.

Crispr Therapeutics has its own in vivo programs, but I cannot fail to be impressed by the progress made by Intellia - a company whose valuation once soared >$10bn when it demonstrated that NTLA-2001, the predecessor to NTLA-2002, was able to reduce serum TTR in patients when delivered in-vivo.

The market has waited a long time for Intellia to build on that historic breakthrough, and dumped Intellia stock along the way, so that today's market cap is just over $1bn.

For me, this feels too low, even accounting for some of the risks in play, i.e., Phase 3 data is not available for either of the lead therapies, comparisons against current standards of care are notoriously unreliable, markets are competitive, funding is being rapidly eroded, study success is not guaranteed.

Provided it is safe and efficacy bars are met, while patients may be suspicious of gene editing therapies, the convenience of being injection / pill free for the rest of their lives, and potentially being free from swelling episodes in HAE, or surviving for longer with fewer cardiovascular complications, will inevitably lead to strong demand for Intellia's drugs, in my view. After all, patients are already embracing gene silencing therapies such as Amvuttra, and Nex-z feels like a logical next step.

There are intriguing catalysts upcoming in 2025, with longer term Phase 1/2 study data for both lead candidates, and as mentioned, since its May drop on what looks like an isolated safety adverse event, quickly resolved, the share price has built some good momentum.

I don't see why that momentum cannot continue, and if the data presentations support what has already been shared, and crystallize the bull case for approvals in three multi-billion dollar markets, I believe the market will respond by buying Intellia and pushing up the share price.

It's been a tough few years for Intellia, but so far as I can see management are doing the right things, and there is a decent chance the company becomes the first to secure approval for an in-vivo gene therapy.

That's why I'd be fairly confident Intellia stock has a lot more upside to capture in the coming years - therefore I'd make its shares a genuinely intriguing long-term buy and hold opportunity with triple digit percentage upside in play - with the caveat that such an investment is not without risk, as I hope I have discussed in this post.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Buy Like Buffett
data.description:
Article Thesis

Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.A)(BRK.B) most recent 13F disclosure shows that the company bought a stake worth more than $1 billion in health insurance giant UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH). The market reacted positively to this news, as this can be seen as a vote of confidence by one of the largest investors in the world -- Berkshire Hathaway, led by investment legend Warren Buffett.

Past Coverage

I have written about both UnitedHealth and Berkshire Hathaway in the past here on Seeking Alpha. My most recent article on UNH is from May, roughly three months ago, while I last covered Berkshire Hathaway in April. In this article, I'll focus on UnitedHealth -- what the purchase by Berkshire Hathaway means, and what UNH's recent earnings results tell us about the company.

What Happened?

This week, many of the largest investors in the world announced their purchases and sales during the second quarter. This included Berkshire Hathaway, which was active during the April to June period, buying and selling positions in different companies. One major purchase was UnitedHealth, in which the company bought a little over five million shares during the period. We do not know whether that position is still the same: It is possible that the company added to its position following the end of June, while it is also possible that BRK made some sales since then, although I do not think that such a short holding period would make a lot of sense. Assuming the position is still the same today, BRK's position in UnitedHealth is worth around $1.5 billion today.

The market reacted very positively to this news, with UnitedHealth trading up by around 10%. I don't find this too surprising -- Buffett purchases are oftentimes seen as a vote of confidence, especially when it comes to embattled companies where there is a major bull-bear debate. That holds true for UNH, which had seen its shares drop by more than 50% over the last year, and where around 15 million shares are sold short.

What Does A Berkshire Hathaway Purchase Tell Us?

No one always makes perfect investment decisions, thus there is no guarantee that Berkshire Hathaway is correct in going long UnitedHealth. But the company's track record is excellent -- Berkshire clearly has systems in place that allow it and its leadership to make good investment decisions often.

Warren Buffett and Berkshire Hathaway also have a contrarian approach from time to time. Companies sometimes experience short-term issues without the company being threatened in the long run. This can result in substantial share price declines that later turn out to be temporary only. When one is able to identify that a company has a high chance of recovering, these temporary share price slumps can make great buying opportunities. This is often described as "buying when others are fearful", which seems to be the case with UnitedHealth right now. Investors sold off their positions in UNH due to UNH's current profitability issues, but Berkshire Hathaway does not seem to be fearful -- instead, it looks like they see a buying opportunity in what they (presumably) deem an undervalued quality company currently going through a rough patch. Berkshire Hathaway has, for example, invested in banks like Bank of America (BAC) during the Great Financial Crisis, which is an example of a contrarian investment in a company with temporary issues that ultimately worked out great for Berkshire Hathaway.

Again, there is no guarantee that Berkshire Hathaway is right this time -- but its track record suggests that there is a good chance of that. Warren Buffett, with his contrarian/value/quality approach, likes UnitedHealth following UNH's share price crash over the last year, indicating that the company could indeed be undervalued right now.

UnitedHealth: Recent Performance And Outlook

Let's also take a look at how UnitedHealth has been doing operationally. During the most recent quarter, for which we just got the report a few weeks ago, the company generated appealing business growth: Revenues for Q2 were up by 13% versus one year earlier, which was in line with what the analyst community had expected. Healthcare is a growth industry, so a sizeable revenue increase was not much of a surprise, but it is still worth noting that UnitedHealth's revenue growth rate was better than during the previous five quarters, when UNH recorded a high-single-digit business growth rate.

UnitedHealth's revenue growth was driven by a good performance for both Optum, UNH's pharmacy benefit and tech business, as well as UnitedHealthcare, UNH's insurance business. Thanks to an aging population, some population growth, and a growing portion of GDP going into healthcare, these industries keep growing at a nice pace, and UnitedHealth, as a leading player in this space, benefits from that.

Unfortunately, revenue growth is not the only contributing factor for a company's profits, though. Cost performance is important as well, especially in industries with major cost headwinds. That holds true for healthcare, as drug prices, medical tech prices, wages, and so on keep climbing at a substantial pace. During the second quarter, UnitedHealth saw its costs grow more than its revenues, which is why its margins declined compared to one year earlier. While analysts were already expecting some profit headwinds and lower earnings versus one year earlier, the profit decline still was a little larger than expected. UnitedHealth's net margin dropped to just 3.1% versus 4.2% during the previous year's quarter. While a 110 base point margin decline seems very stomachable, the fact that the company's margin was already rather low one year earlier meant that UNH's 110 base point net margin decline equated to a whopping one-fourth of the company's total profit margin. This made UNH's net profit decline to $3.5 billion for the quarter. It is important to note that this is still around $14 billion a year, so UNH remains pretty profitable in absolute terms, despite being significantly less profitable than one year ago.

Adjusting UNH's profits during the second quarter for one-time expenses and similar items that do not accurately reflect the company's underlying ability to generate profits, things looked better -- adjusted net earnings were $4.08 per share, or around $3.7 billion. For the remainder of the year, analysts currently forecast more or less the same -- the consensus estimate for this year's earnings per share stands at $16.23, roughly four times the adjusted profit generated in Q2. This is around 40% less than in 2024, which is a sizeable decline, but considering the steep share price drop seen over the last year (more than 50%), the current share price more than accounts for that by now.

It is also expected that profitability will improve again next year, due to factors such as medical inflation coming down, Medicare funding improving, and so on. Analysts believe that UNH will see its earnings per share rise by 18% next year and by 15% and 21% in 2027 and 2028, respectively. Even if we assume that these growth estimates are 3% too high for every year, UnitedHealth would earn around $25 per share in 2028, or three years from now, which would be close to the profit seen last year -- and that assumes that current analyst expectations are too bullish.

If UnitedHealth were to earn $25 per share in 2028, its shares could see hefty gains over the next few years. The company's earnings multiple oftentimes was in the low-20s in the past, but even if the earnings multiple were to expand to just 18 three years from now, UNH could trade at around $450 in 2028. For someone buying today, that would mean a ~50% share price gain over three years, with a ~3% annual dividend yield on top of that. For someone who bought before the share price pop that was caused by the Berkshire Hathaway announcement, upside potential is even better, of course. I think that it is not too surprising that Buffett is interested in UNH -- the company clearly has significant upside potential if it can right the ship by improving margins again. If one were to make a more bullish assumption -- ~$27.00 in earnings per share in 2028, in line with the analyst consensus, and a 20x earnings multiple, UNH could climb to around $540 three years from now, which would mean gains of around 80% over that time frame, not considering the dividend.

Is UNH A Buy?

I don't think that buying solely because Berkshire Hathaway bought UNH is a great idea, but Buffett's investment strengthens the bull case. UNH is experiencing headwinds right now due to steep cost increases and Medicare issues, but those can be solved. If UNH is able to solve these, shares could generate pretty attractive returns over the coming years -- and since UNH's long-term track record is positive, I would not be surprised to see UNH tackle its current problems successfully.

For someone with a multi-year investment horizon who is not afraid of some risk and volatility, I think UNH looks attractive.

data.title: 2 CEF Swaps Into ETFs For July 2025
data.description:
Every month, I share what I'm doing within my closed-end fund portfolio, and that hasn't changed. However, my investments are not just into closed-end funds and other individual stocks that I share at Cash Builder Opportunities. I also hold several ETFs, which I've covered in the past and have been looking to increase my allocation to ETFs potentially.

So with that, this month was a bit different as I swapped out two CEFs in favor of two ETFs. I wanted to provide a specific focus on the moves I made and provide the reasoning for why I made the swaps. Overall, this was repositioning my overall investments a bit as they weren't exactly swaps of equals, which we tend to do with CEFs.

BRW To CEFS

An initial swapping was Saba Capital Income & Opportunities Fund (BRW) in favor of Saba's ETF, the Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS). Saba Capital Management manages both; however, CEFS participates more in the activist campaigns that they often run against CEFs. Below are the top holdings listed for CEFS.

BRW also participates, but to a much smaller degree. BRW is mostly just a portfolio run by Boaz, including individual equity and fixed-income positions, as well as participating in shorting. Below is the BRW top ten holdings, which we can see are starkly different from the above.

With that, I think the track record has become quite clear that Boaz is a better activist than he is a portfolio manager. Of course, the usual caveat exists here; this is only past performance, and there is no guarantee that this can continue to be the case. It's possible that Saba was mostly just lucky with their activist campaigns and less lucky with their underlying portfolio investment strategy in BRW.

However, that wasn't the only nudge that I needed to make the swap between these two funds. The other decision determining factor for making the switch was that BRW's discount has narrowed quite meaningfully lately, taking away the main advantage of owning BRW over CEFS—the discount/premium opportunity where CEFs can swing wildly from their underlying NAV price.

With this shift, I actually sold a CEF in favor of an ETF, but my overall investment exposure to CEFs would have expanded in this move. CEFS is highly diversified thanks to its fund-of-funds approach. That also includes higher expenses as you have fees on fees, but the total returns listed above include that as well.

The distributions for these funds are both paid monthly, with BRW paying 12.90% currently and CEFS at 8.41%. Definitely a significant difference, but an actual distribution rate has never been a determining factor in my investment decisions—mostly as long as it yields something. Both of these rates are still relatively high, regardless.

GLO To QDTE

The other swap I made was from Clough Global Opportunities Fund (GLO) to adding to my already held Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE). This was a move in what I consider to be my "High-Yield Reacher Portfolio." An account that I've started to transition into all the super yielders from NEOS, RoundHill, YieldMax and others.

Something that I'm looking to expand more into further as opportunities arise, but with a stretched market, there is no rush. A few names that I'm looking to include are some of those that I've been looking at and covering quite recently: NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI) and NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI).

That said, I picked up a position several years ago in GLO when the fund had a terrible performing year in 2022, and based on their managed distribution policy of 10% saw the distribution payout adjusted massively.

As usually tends to be the case when that occurs, the fund dropped to a deep discount. A deep discount wasn't that unusual for the fund; what was more unusual was the premium the fund had been trading at leading up to that period.

My logic was that investors may push the fund back up to a narrower discount or even a premium again. The premium certainly hasn't occurred, and the discount narrowing was quite limited. That said, I still got out with a sizeable gain as the following years after the 2022 drop were met with strong total returns anyway. This was mostly paid out through the fund's distribution.

GLO is more of a hybrid fund that isn't restricted to any specific area, including equities, fixed-income, global investments, and even shorting investments on occasion. They are leveraged as well through borrowings, which means any moves are going to be amplified.

QDTE, on the other hand, is very much specifically focused on the Nasdaq 100 exposure. They attain this through long call options, then they have an overwrite strategy where they write 0 days-to-expiration options as well. That's how they achieve such a high distribution rate.

Meaning the swap here would have altered my overall investments to some degree to become heavier in equities and more specifically, tilted even more toward the tech sector. A space that seems quite hot, thanks to the AI revolution that seems to be getting pretty hyped up. We're seeing quite stretched valuations, and with such a focus on tech now in the broader market, such as the Nasdaq and S&P 500 Indexes, it's why we are getting to new all-time highs regularly.

How long that momentum can keep up, I'm not sure. Which goes back to the fact that I'm in no rush to add aggressively to some names at this time, instead making more of these swap trades where I'm staying invested but not putting too much extra cash to work.

Selling out of GLO was mostly becoming impatient with the discount to narrow. Being that it is a leveraged fund as well, I'm not so sure I really increased risk too meaningfully, as they also carry exposure to several of the same Mag 7 names—albeit at lower overall percentage allocations.

QDTE's distribution is paid weekly while GLO pays monthly. As noted, GLO pays a managed distribution policy that resets at 10% of NAV annually. The current distribution rate for the fund works out to 11.13% based on the share price. QDTE's payout rate is certainly higher at 41.20%. However, I would expect share price erosion over time, as that sort of payout isn't realistic to expect.

Still, the total NAV returns favor QDTE thanks to its heavier tech allocation since its inception. GLO was able to keep up on a total share price return basis, due to the discount narrowing of the fund.

Conclusion

I've sold out of BRW and GLO in the month of July in favor of CEFS and QDTE, two ETFs that are quite different from what I was holding. That said, I think these moves made sense as BRW has seen its discount narrow quite materially. GLO remains trading at a deep discount on an absolute basis, but has narrowed from the depths it had previously seen. I originally expected the discount to narrow a bit quicker, and that didn't happen—at this point, this ~12% discount appears to be the new normal for the fund.

data.title: The Market May Have Hit Its Liquidity Breaking Point
data.description:
The liquidity buffer supporting the market since 2022 has come to an end, with the reverse repurchase facility at the Fed falling to under $30 billion as of August 15, its lowest level since early 2021. Effectively, all the excess liquidity that was sloshing around across the market since the end of 2022 is now gone. We have now reached the point in the cycle where Quantitative Tightening, for as long as it should continue, will have a real impact on reserve balances held at the Fed.

The immediate issue is that the Treasury General Account is still in the process of rising back to $850 billion. As of August 13, the TGA stood at $515 billion, which means there is still another $335 billion to go before the refill is complete. This will be drawn directly from reserve balances held at the Fed and should be completed by quarter-end, if not sooner.

By the time the TGA is completely refilled, reserve balances are expected to decrease from their current level of $3.3 trillion on August 13 to approximately $3 trillion. This is likely to put a strain on liquidity conditions in the market and raise the overnight funding rates. These funding rates could become particularly volatile around month and quarter ends.

Higher Funding Costs

The overnight general collateral rate and the secured overnight financing rate have been trending higher since early June, and with Treasury settlements occurring on a regular basis, it is likely that the overnight funding rate will continue to trend higher. These measures of general collateral and SOFR will serve as the primary gauge of whether liquidity is becoming tight. When the rate falls, it means that liquidity is ample; when these rates rise, it means liquidity is tight. SOFR is published daily at 8:00 a.m. by the New York Fed.

The Fed has a backstop to ensure that these overnight rates don't spiral out of control, as they did in the fall of 2019. The Fed has the Standing Repo Facility, through which it provides liquidity in exchange for collateral when an institution needs overnight funding. That facility kicks in if rates climb over 4.5%. This facility has experienced minimal usage over the years, with its highest participation rate reaching just $11.1 billion as of June 30, 2025, when the GC rate spiked on quarter-end. This data is published every day at 1:45 PM ET by the New York Fed.

A New Source of Liquidity Will Be Needed

With the Treasury slated to issue a deluge of debt between now and year-end, the funding for that debt will need to come from somewhere else. The buffer the reverse repo facility provided is now likely to be drawn from other parts of the market.

This is precisely why the TGA refill in 2023 didn't result in a significant drain on liquidity and reserve balance, as the Reserve Repo facility provided the financing for that issuance. This time will be different from that perspective. With the reverse repo effectively drained, liquidity will first have to come from reserves, and then, subsequently, from private balance sheets, such as those of primary dealers, money market funds, and banks.

We have reached a liquidity inflection point for the marketplace. The end of the reverse repo facility means the end of the easy liquidity conditions and low overnight financing rates. This is likely to be a point in time where market volatility for risk assets increases as liquidity tightens.

It may also be the point at which the theory of fiscal dominance comes to an end and instead becomes a period of fiscal strain.

data.title: Williams Companies: Stuck At Overvalued
data.description:
Williams Companies (NYSE: NYSE:WMB) is one of the largest midstream companies, worth more than $70 billion. It runs the legendary Transco pipeline, one of the most important pipeline systems in the U.S., with numerous bolt-on opportunities. Despite that importance, and a >20% underperformance since we recommend against investing, a lofty valuation, with a dividend less than 3.5%, makes the company a poor investment.

Williams Companies Growth

The company managed to achieve 8% YoY growth in adjusted EBITDA YoY.

The company benefited from higher Transco rates along with some minor expansions. The company also saw strength in its Northeast G&P division and West divisions where it's had the ability to bolt onto its existing assets, especially in the Northeast G&P segment. The high single-digit growth here is quite impressive.

Financially, the company's YTD adjusted EBITDA growth was 5% resulting in 5% growth in EPS. However, the company's AFFO stayed flat and the company's dividend coverage ratio declined by 5%. The company has a 3.46% dividend yield indicating a 7.8% AFFO yield, a relatively low yield that limits the company's ability to continue returns.

At the same time, the company has ramped up capital to $3.4 billion annualized, leaving it with $1 billion not enough to fully cover its dividend. That's resulted in the company's debt-to-adjusted EBITDA increasing to 3.80x. The company's almost $29 billion in debt is extremely manageable but it does come with roughly $2 billion / year in interest expenses.

Williams Companies Accomplishments

The company has had a minor guidance increase, which is respectable.

The company has increased its adjusted EBITDA guidance at the midpoint by a mere $50 million, but any increase is reasonable. The company also completed a small acquisition of Saber Midstream ($43 million) but it is an effective bolt-on acquisition. The company is continuing to look at bolt-on opportunities with transco and deepwater in the Gulf.

The company's updated guidance is for 6% growth in AFFO and 5% growth in AFFO per share. The company expects it'll be able to keep its dividend coverage ratio flat. The company is expecting roughly $2.7 billion in growth capex and $850 million in maintenance capex for $3.5 billion in total capital expenditures.

The company's AFFO will be ~$2.1 billion post capital expenditures which means the company will be ~$350 million short in cash post dividends. Now this is a dividend level that the company can afford and the company is guiding for 5.3% growth, but it's slower YoY growth and we expect that growth rate to remain low.

Williams Companies Valuation

To indicate the weakness in Williams Companies' valuation, we're taking a look at Enbridge, another major midstream company.

These number are in Canadian $ (0.73 USD / CAD). Still the $105 billion USD company is expecting $15.3 billion USD in adjusted EBITDA and $9.8 billion in adjusted DCF. That's a 9.3% DCF yield and a 14.6% adjusted EBITDA load. That's versus 8% and 11% respectively for Williams Companies. Enbridge's 8% adjusted EBITDA growth is in line with Williams Companies.

One can argue Enbridge has more debt but that's managed through the interest expenses before DCF. Enbridge's substantially lower valuation shows its opportunity.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the company's incredibly impressive Transco pipeline infrastructure and the associated add-on acquisitions that the company has been able to undergo. This combination of infrastructure enables the company to continue growing its cash flow which could enable the company to grow into its valuation, making it a valuable investment.

Conclusion

Williams Companies has an impressive portfolio of assets centered around the Transco pipeline which moves millions of barrels per day of oil. The company operates as a toll operator for this incredibly impressive pipeline system earning cash while having numerous additional bolt-on opportunities for the pipeline.

Despite that, the company trades at a lofty valuation. The company's 3.5% dividend yield is lower than many midstream peers, and while the company is investing in growth, growth rates aren't stunningly high. Versus a peer like Enbridge, the company is overvalued, making it a poor investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Firm U.S. Retail Activity And Elevated Import Costs Weaken 50bp Fed Cut Calls
data.description:
By James Knightley

Import prices suggest US firms are bearing the burden of tariffs

Casting further doubt on the "foreigners will pay" claim from the Administration, July import prices rose 0.4% month-on-month, while ex petroleum they were up 0.3% versus expectations of 0.1% MoM increases for both. Tempering the news is some downward revisions to June of 0.2 percentage points to both, to -0.1% and -0.2% respectively. Nonetheless, the data overall suggests that US importers are bearing the burden of tariffs so far and that at some point this will result in higher retail prices for goods.

Core inflation measures YoY%

That could change in time, but it will take time to negotiate any price cuts with foreign producers, and there is likely to be some reluctance to acquiesce as they are already feeling pain from dollar weakness. US contracts are normally priced in dollars, so unhedged foreign firms will be earning less in their local currencies.

Mixed news for retailers as sentiment remains subdued

Separately, retail sales rose 0.5% MoM versus the 0.6% consensus forecasts, but June's growth rate was revised up to 0.9% from 0.6% initially reported. The details show autos rose 1.6% with furniture sales up 1.4% and non-store retail (boosted by Amazon Prime Day sales) up 0.8% with clothing up 0.7%. Electronics sales fell for a third consecutive month though (this time by 0.6%), building materials were down 1%, miscellaneous dropped 1.7%, and eating, drinking out fell 0.4%. So it is a very mixed performance among the sectors.

Retail sales account for just over 40% of total consumer spending, and here there has been an overall cooling, which is in line with weaker consumer sentiment readings. Concern about tariff-induced price hikes and worries about the prospect for the jobs market do appear to be causing a slowing in spending growth, but there is little sign of any recession threat right now.

Consumer confidence and real consumer spending (YoY%)

US manufacturing not benefiting from tariffs... yet...

US industrial production fell 0.1% MoM in July versus the 0% consensus, while June's growth rate was revised up to 0.4% from 0.3%. Manufacturing output was flat on the month after a 0.3% increase in June. There is little sign of the sector benefiting from a switching to US-made products on the back of tariffs, but again, contracts take time to negotiate.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: A Concrete Reason To Wait On XLK
data.description:
The Technology Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLK) represents the S&P 500 sector with the highest momentum and among those with the largest positive EPS revision (2025-2026). Yet this has led to a critical issue: record relative valuations.

I try to maintain a pragmatic approach: while it is true that momentum is an increasingly vital ingredient for successful investments, I believe it cannot disregard a basic logic of valuation. Simply put, I think XLK is too expensive.

First, however... you need to know that…

XLK is a very representative ETF of the info tech sector

With an AUM of $85.7B, an expense ratio of 0.08% and its 71 holdings, XLK is considered by many the most representative ETF of the technology sector.

And this is a characteristic of the SPDR sector ETFs: they are all quite concentrated in terms of holdings, but at the same time very representative of the sector. It is the same with XLK: the top 10 alone represent over 65% of the portfolio, led by NVDA, Microsoft and Apple, which alone account for about 42%.

And in fact this is the main criticism made of XLK. However, if we see it in technical terms, in the end it maintains a similar sharpe ratio to more fragmented ETFs like VGT (an alternative to XLK that I appreciate), an ETF with 322 holdings, therefore about 4x more.

Strong Momentum

In my latest coverage of the tech market, I have been quite bullish, especially after the April-May 2025 crash over tariff fears. Today the market has recovered, and it is in a phase of strong technical momentum.

A phase well highlighted by the quant rating, which signals BUY, and the SA grade, which gives a nice A to the momentum box for XLK.

What has pushed valuations higher? What a question! Earnings… or rather, expectations about them. Info tech today prices in a 19% expected earnings growth for 2025, whereas in June it was 17%.

So the guidance from tech companies has revised expectations upward in the midst of one of the most uncertain moments in recent history, and the market knows this is not a given. Even looking at 2026, the prospects are equally bright, with more than 18% expected earnings growth, up from 17.8% in June.

Valuations no.

Not surprisingly, XLK is among the most attractive sectors for tactical investors according to BofA’s quant team (August 2025) for Price momentum and EPS estimate revisions.

Problem? In the valuation section, it is instead among the lowest. In other words, XLK is among the most expensive. Being a momentum/earnings-driven ETF, the increase in earnings expectations and the explosion of the “risk on” sentiment in the market have brought “traffic” to XLK. But, following the narrative of the last 30 years of relative valuations, perhaps “too much traffic.” And this is quite clear: the FWD P/E of the U.S. tech sector (S&P 500) is at the 85th percentile of the 30-year distribution. It has practically had more extreme valuations only during the dot-com bubble. And I know that said like this it sounds a lot like a classic “alarmist headline,” but, at least for 2025, forward valuations are moving toward thirty-year highs, and as much as I like to be optimistic, it is something that must be taken into account.

Personal opinion

How do I move? In the short term, it may be interesting to try working on flash recoveries from the lows (because there is strong momentum), but for long-term operations, I think the right rating in my opinion is HOLD. In other words, be patient. In the sense that I see it as a moment in which those who have taken a position are enjoying the fruits/results. Those who are out are at high risk of “FOMO.”

But be careful: in my opinion, this does not at all justify a SELL rating. Info tech is one of the 3 sectors of the S&P 500 with the highest earnings growth prospects. A fundamental ingredient for the growth of an earnings-driven sector like technology.

Risk

Personally, I strongly disagree with the idea that this is a bubble, in the style of the dot-com bubble. As of today, I would rather read it as an imbalance to be adjusted. Why? Simply because the essential ingredient is missing: a cut in earnings growth expectations. Here expectations are bullish.

When would it become something close to a bubble? Simply when earnings fall. And I ask myself: What could be the catalyst that would generate a contraction in expected earnings such that the fwd P/E becomes truly unsustainable? The catalyst could be a price increase from NVDA (in my opinion), a narrative still excluded from the market’s discussion table. Why NVDA? First of all because NVDA alone weighs more than 15% in XLK, but above all because NVDA’s business is tied to massive orders from large hyperscalers (Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, Alphabet, etc. ).

And it is a risk, especially when considering the ever-increasing verticalization of activity in the U.S., especially after the potential export tax to China of about 13%. To this is added the impact of tariffs, still high rates and rising inflation. Therefore, I closely monitor NVDA as a risk for XLK.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think the right rating for XLK is HOLD. It is a very representative ETF of the tech sector, and it is going through a phase of EPS expansion and it has strong positive momentum. But this has driven forward valuations to particularly high levels in relative terms.

data.title: Vanguard S&P 500 Growth Index Fund: VOO With A Growth Tilt
data.description:
The highest growth half of VOO

In today's article, we are going to be examining the Vanguard S&P 500 Growth Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA: NYSEARCA:VOOG). Many investors are familiar with The Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) yet may be unaware that a version of the fund tilted toward growth stocks by Vanguard also exists. Most investors tend to default to the popular Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) when making their growth allocations, but there are actually several choices.

Here are some examples of growth ETFs across some of the largest ETF issuers:

Where Does VOOG Fit, And Who Is It Best Suited For?

From Vanguard:

Product summary

-Invests in stocks in the Standard & Poor’s 500 Growth Index, composed of the growth companies in the S&P 500.

-Focuses on closely tracking the index’s return, which is considered a gauge of overall U.S. growth stock returns.

-Offers high potential for investment growth; share value rises and falls more sharply than that of funds holding bonds.

-More appropriate for long-term goals where your money’s growth is essential.

When it comes to "investment style" categories of index funds that adhere to growth or value, these are the types of funds for do-it-yourselfers that may also have a financial planner. Sometimes that financial planner is fee based and receives both fees for services and assets under management plus a commission for products, like mutual funds or life insurance products that the planner sells you from affiliated parties. Other times they are fee only and may or may not manage funds for you in separately managed accounts [SMA]. With more and more individuals just choosing to pay a flat fee for a financial plan regarding their investment portfolios, that plan is most likely to have an allocation for growth, equity blend and value depending on the stage of wealth accumulation the individual is currently at in life.

In a typical scenario, the manager might put you in a growth mutual fund with active management and one that is value oriented according to your age or risk tolerance level. This type of growth aspect in VOOG allows you the investor to make a simple allocation to the "growth" category in a low cost, not actively managed fashion. This saves the investor on management fees and tax drag that happens when a mutual fund actively buys and sells shares. ETFs, which are typically passive and follow an index, are both low cost and do not try to beat their target index. These funds simply seek to replicate the index they follow without selling shares unless an index rebalances or removes a member from the index and replaces it with a new entrant.

An example of style investing that a financial planner may recommend as general advice, would be exhibited above. As many planners can not recommend individual securities, including specific index funds, without certain licenses based on the investors tailored situation, these types of recommendations would be common in a general fee only financial plan for a one time fee.

The Index The Vanguard S&P 500 Growth Index Fund Follows

First off, the basics of this index fund. The fund has a modified market cap weighting system which we will examine shortly. The fund rebalances once annually in December at the end of each year and quarterly throughout the year. The general goal of the index is to overweight stocks from the S&P 500 (SP500) that have high sales growth, a high ratio of earnings change relative to the stock's price and strong momentum. Momentum has to do with a stock's tendency to continue in a positive upward direction and is usually measured using a relative strength index [RSI] or other technical indicators.

As with most growth strategies over the last decade, most growth indexes and growth mutual fund styles have done better than the S&P 500 on a total return basis. This however also comes with higher volatility. Making an assessment of an investor's own risk tolerance important in determining the amount of allocation an investor wants to dedicate to a growth style. It's not always as simple as saying this methodology outperforms, so why would we do anything else? Investor psychology and being able to stay in the market is equally as important.

In a decade long backtest of VOO vs VOOG, we can see that VOOG outperforms by 2.14% on an annualized CAGR from January 1, 2016 to current. The drawdowns are also much larger. In VOOG's worst drawdown the fund retreated -30.47% and its worst year was -29.48%. This is in comparison to VOO's max drawdown of -23.91% and worst year of -18.19%. These are all total return figures that include both capital appreciation/depreciation plus the dividend.

If an investor is actively living off of their portfolio income, higher volatility can sometimes lead to slightly lower success rates in Monte Carlo portfolio survival scenarios. This is due to the possibility of bigger drawdowns that hurt the capital value when selling shares at the bottom than would happen with a less volatile strategy. This is the reason why a variety of baskets are recommended in financial planning.

The portfolio tends to be very overweight the Information Technology sector (XLK) as represented by the teal slice in the pie chart. This higher concentration to one sector is what makes the investment style more volatile.

If we compare this with the S&P 500, Information Technology is only a 34% weight by comparison.

How the weighting works

Unlike the S&P 500 which is an un-capped index, the capped growth and value indexes that S&P Dow Jones Indices offer do have a capping methodology with rebalancing. They describe it in detail below:

Constituent Weighting. At each rebalancing, each index is capped market capitalization weighted using the following process:

1. With prices reflected on the rebalancing reference date, adjusted for any applicable corporate actions, and membership, shares outstanding, IWFs, and Growth/Value Factor as of the rebalancing effective date, weight each company by style adjusted FMC.

2. If any company weighs more than 24% cap the company’s weight at 23%, which allows for a 2% buffer.

3. Proportionally redistribute any excess weight to all uncapped companies within the relevant index. The process continues iteratively until no company breaches the 23% weight cap.

4. The aggregate weight of companies with weights greater than 4.8% cannot exceed 50% of the total index weight.

5. If the rule in step 4 is breached, rank all companies in descending order by weight and reduce the weight of the smallest company whose weight is greater than 4.8% that causes the step 4 breach to 4.5%. Continue the process iteratively until step 4 is satisfied.

6. Assign index share amounts to each constituent to arrive at the weights calculated above.

7. If, on the third to last business day of March, June, September, or December, a company has an index weight greater than 24%, or the sum of the companies with weights greater than 4.8% exceeds 50%, a secondary reweighting is triggered with the rebalancing effective date after the close of the last business day of the month with any applicable reweighting changes appearing in daily proforma and corporate action files starting on the second to last business day of the month. The secondary reweighting uses the closing prices as of the third to last business day of March, June, September, or December, and membership, shares outstanding, IWFs and Growth/Value Factor as of the rebalancing effective date.

While the rebalancing methodology is a bit complex, the gist of the concept here is that the S&P 500 constituents will float freely with their growth and market capitalizations while the "style" investing indexes like VOOG will not allow one company within the index to grow beyond a certain percentage of the portfolio weight. The total amount of holdings within the index are also fewer than the S&P 500 which has 500 holdings. This index currently has 212 constituents. With stricter qualifications, the S&P 500 Growth index disqualifies more than half of the S&P 500.

Summary

The Vanguard S&P 500 Growth Index Fund ETF is appropriate for investors trying to achieve a specific financial plan with guidelines that adhere to certain investing baskets and investment styles. These styles are normally growth, value and blended. Keep in mind that the closer an investor is to the asset preservation and gifting stages of their investment lifecycles, growth baskets become less and less appropriate due to their volatility. The main goal when living off portfolio income, gifting it or putting it in a trust for future generations is to reduce volatility and make sure there is no liquidity crisis in case of a bear market.

On the other hand, for the younger investor with more time ahead to accumulate W2 derived earned income, an ETF like VOOG may be appropriate in greater proportions to the value and blended baskets as the investor has longer before they need to rely on their portfolio for income purposes.

This article answers these three main questions about VOOG:

  • What type of investor is VOOG best suited for?
  • Is VOOG more volatile than the average ETF?
  • Does VOOG carry sector concentration risks?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Archer Aviation: Leaving No Room For Disappointment
data.description:
Archer Aviation: Needs To Convert Growth Prospects Into Revenue

It seems like speculative investors just refuse to give up, riding on the tech-driven advance that has driven the S&P 500 (SPX, SPY) to new highs in August. The stars have also started to align for the leading eVTOL companies like Archer Aviation Inc. (NYSE:ACHR), paving the way toward commercialization in the U.S. and in international markets.

With a growing order book spanning beyond the domestic market opportunity, Archer Aviation appears to be priming for an accelerated scale up and deployment phase. The beaming prospects could see the company capture substantive benefits from a burgeoning customer base keen on leveraging the nascent, but high-growth industry, offering ACHR lucrative first mover advantages into what is a tightly regulated opportunity with multiple layers of certification, helping to establish a robust and defensible moat.

In my last Archer Aviation writeup, I articulated the need to remain circumspect even though I appreciated the upcoming commercial launches, while expanding its reach into the defense industrial base with capable and well-connected partners.

Archer Aviation: Timeline For Full Certification Will Be Scrutinized

Well, the good news is that ACHR's bullish narrative seems to have stayed on the right track. I believe the certification process remains the main bugbear in hampering even more aggressive buying sentiments from bullish investors. Accordingly, management articulated that while they are still working closely with the FAA in "conducting reviews," the compliance completion process has reached the 15% approval mark. Hence, execution risks on the exact timing for the full approval cannot be fully ascertained, although management doesn't appear to be concerned about not being able to obtain the necessary certification.

Archer Aviation has highlighted that it's also ramping up in preparation for the LA28 Olympics, compressing the timeline for achieving full FAA certification to about the 1,000-day mark. Hence, the battle with arch-rival Joby Aviation (JOBY) could define the opening eVTOL economics in the hotly contested domestic market, given the multibillion order book that Archer Aviation has already secured in the U.S.

Hence, I believe investors must pay close attention to crucial phases in its U.S. launch program, with critical junctures including TIA flight testing as one of the pivotal milestones that the company is aiming to secure in the near term. In addition, the ability to attain the production certificate will also be another crucial checkpoint in its certification ramp up program, based on the current FAA inspection underway for its Silicon Valley and Georgia manufacturing facilities.

Hence, I think it's opportune to know that ACHR's commercial opportunity is predicated on its ability to capture the emerging eVTOL economics beyond the shores of the U.S. And the primary UAE launch market appears to be pacing well for a further scale-up, having already delivered its first Midnight aircraft while also conducted tests in the harsh July weather.

Notably, the company also emphasized that the current initiative with Abu Dhabi Aviation is expected to gain further traction in the next 18 to 24 months, with scheduled milestone payments worth "tens of millions of dollars" starting from the second half of 2025. So, I think it does show that a solidly successful UAE launch effort could help mitigate investor worries about delayed FAA final phase certification efforts that could stymie buying sentiments at a crucial juncture.

In addition, the fledgling international launch program predicated on the partnerships with Ethiopia, and Indonesia should also help provide early revenue accretion efforts, while helping to validate its capabilities to other launch markets in the future.

Buy Or Sell ACHR Stock?

As a result, I believe the optimistic modeling by Wall Street analysts on a rapid acceleration in revenue through the 2029 forecast period seems reasonable, given the multi-year scaling framework promulgated by management. However, it's also critical to note that while the $1.7B in liquidity might be sufficient to tank the $1.44B in cash burn through FY2028, it also depends very much on the scale up economics from 2026, pending FAA certification.

Consider that the company's growing order book also consists of a $1.5B deal with United Airlines, with a program spanning across multiple key hubs. However, it's crucial to note that backlog doesn't automatically convert into revenue, suggesting that it still depends on overcoming the various obstacles (and not just certification) that could still hamper a more confident ramp up in the next few years.

With an FY2029 revenue multiple of 3.6x already markedly more than the sector's median on a forward basis of 1.67x, I believe the market has already baked in substantial optimism on a multi-year rollout. Hence, unless management provides much higher growth prospects with a more significant backlog through the FY2029 forecast period, it might not be enough to spur much more aggressive buying, if the execution falters, or unanticipated delays encumbered Archer Aviation's multiple launch programs.

With that in mind, I still view the risk/reward as unfavorable for investors thinking about entering Archer Aviation Inc. stock now. I exhort them to demonstrate more patience for the company to work through the certification progress, while also biding their time for a more pronounced pullback before deciding whether to pull the buy trigger.

Rating: Maintain Hold.
